doc.title: Classify the cash flows
doc.event: Jara Co. had these events: collected from customers; paid suppliers and employees; bought equipment; sold an investment; issued bonds; paid dividends; converted bonds into stock. Classify each as operating, investing, financing — or a noncash disclosure.

variants.label: The three buckets + the footnote bucket

computationPaths.narration: The sorting rule: OPERATING = income-statement life (customers, suppliers, employees, interest, taxes). INVESTING = long-term ASSETS moving (buy/sell PP&E, investments, loans made). FINANCING = long-term CAPITAL moving (debt issued/repaid, stock issued/bought back, dividends PAID). No cash at all → noncash investing/financing, disclosed in a note, never on the statement face.

memorize.body: GAAP's odd couples: interest PAID and interest/dividends RECEIVED = operating (they're on the income statement); dividends PAID = financing. Memorize the asymmetry — it's tested constantly.
No cash, no statement: stock-for-land, bonds-to-stock conversions, capital-lease signings are NONCASH — a separate note, not a line item in any section.

questions.prompt: Dividends PAID to shareholders are classified as…

distractors.feedback: Operating is where dividends RECEIVED go. Dividends PAID are a return to capital providers — FINANCING. (Interest paid, oddly, stays operating.)
